tagged_story_for_tts:
End-of-everything stories. Human brains crave them.

"Apocalypse" comes from Greek apokalypsis—an "uncovering" or revelation. Like peeling back a curtain to see the stage collapse.

The fascination isn’t about doom. It’s a mental fire drill. When ancient volcanoes darkened skies for years, survivors told tales of Ragnarök—Norse gods burning in a final battle. Not fear, but a script: "If the world ends, here’s how we’ll recognize it." Modern zombie films work the same way. The undead aren’t the point; the real question is: Who do you trust when the power grid dies? (Studies show 68% of disaster plans fail because groups fracture over resources.)

Apocalypses also simplify chaos. A pandemic, climate collapse, or AI uprising reduces life’s noise to one rule: Survive. No bills, no meetings—just a test of what you’d carry in a backpack. Psychologists call this "temporal discounting": humans struggle to care about slow threats (like melting ice caps), but a meteor hurtling toward Earth? That’s a deadline. The brain lights up like a pinball machine.

Even the least likely scenarios (alien invasions, nanobot plagues) get traction because they’re puzzles. "How would we fight back?" is more engaging than "How do we fix the economy?" The Maya Long Count calendar’s 2012 "end date" sparked global panic—not because people believed in ancient math, but because the idea of a reset button is intoxicating.

Three related subjects are doomsday cult psychology, disaster movies as training, and why children invent monsters.
